QF.id: XIII Introduction to Forwards and Futures_43

question.text: What is 'basis risk' and why can't it be eliminated completely even with futures hedging?

answer.text: Basis risk is the risk that the spot-futures price relationship changes unpredictably — it can't be eliminated because futures contracts are standardized (fixed lot, expiry) while underlying exposures may differ in timing, quantity, or exact specification
Basis risk can always be eliminated by choosing the correct futures contract
Basis risk only exists in commodity markets where physical delivery causes uncertainty
Basis risk is the same as market risk — the risk that prices move against your position

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Basis risk persists because: (1) Expiry mismatch — hedge may end before futures expiry, (2) Quantity mismatch — lot sizes may not match exposure exactly, (3) Asset mismatch — underlying may not be identical (e.g., hedging a stock with index futures), (4) Basis at close of hedge is uncertain. **The Trap:** No hedge is perfect unless the underlying and the derivative match exactly in every dimension. Basis risk is residual risk AFTER hedging, not market risk itself. **Cross-Exam Context:** Basis risk is universal across all derivative markets — equity cross-hedging (VIII), currency cross-rate hedging (I), duration-based bond hedging (IV), and commodity location basis (XVI). **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Minimize basis risk by: (1) matching contract expiry to hedge horizon, (2) choosing the most correlated futures contract, (3) adjusting for beta/duration/notional as appropriate.
